question_en: How many elements does the periodic table currently have?
context_en: The periodic table has 118 confirmed elements. The last to be added was Oganesson (Og, no. 118), in 2016.

question_en: How fast does light travel in a vacuum?
context_en: Light travels at 299,792,458 m/s (~300,000 km/s). This constant 'c' is the speed limit of the universe according to Einstein's relativity.

question_en: Who proposed evolution by natural selection?
context_en: Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species' in 1859. Alfred Wallace reached similar conclusions in parallel.

question_en: What is a diamond made of?
context_en: A diamond is pure carbon in a cubic structure formed under high pressure. It is the hardest natural material (10 on the Mohs scale).

question_en: How many bones does the adult human body have?
context_en: The adult has 206 bones. At birth we have ~300, which fuse as we grow. The smallest is the stapes and the longest is the femur.

question_en: What is the largest planet in the solar system?
context_en: Jupiter is the largest planet: its mass is 2.5 times the sum of all the other planets. It has at least 95 known moons.

question_en: Which scientist formulated the theory of relativity?
context_en: Einstein formulated special relativity in 1905 and general relativity in 1915. E=mc² is the most famous equation in physics.

question_en: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
context_en: Gold is represented as Au, from the Latin 'aurum'. It is a precious metal with atomic number 79, widely used in jewelry and electronics.

question_en: Which planet is known as the 'red planet'?
context_en: Mars is the red planet because of its surface rich in iron oxide. It has the largest volcanoes in the solar system (Olympus Mons).

question_en: How many chromosomes does a normal human cell have?
context_en: Human cells have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs). Gametes (sperm and eggs) have 23. Down syndrome involves 47.

question_en: Who discovered penicillin?
context_en: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 by noticing that a fungus (Penicillium) killed bacteria on his Petri dish.

question_en: What is the chemical formula for water?
context_en: Water is H₂O: two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen. It is the only compound that naturally exists in all three states of matter.

question_en: What is photosynthesis?
options_en: The process by which plants produce oxygen and glucose
context_en: Photosynthesis converts CO₂ and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight. It occurs in the chloroplasts thanks to chlorophyll.

question_en: How many planets are there in the solar system?
context_en: Since 2006, the solar system has 8 planets: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune. Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet.

question_en: What is DNA?
options_en: The molecule that contains genetic information
context_en: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) contains the genetic instructions of all living beings. Its double-helix structure was discovered in 1953.

question_en: What is the most abundant element in the universe?
context_en: Hydrogen makes up 75% of the normal matter in the universe. Helium is the second. Both formed in the Big Bang.

question_en: At what temperature does water boil at sea level?
context_en: Water boils at 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm). At higher altitude, pressure is lower and it boils at a lower temperature.

question_en: Who was Marie Curie?
options_en: The first person to win two Nobel Prizes
context_en: Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911). She discovered polonium and radium. She was the first woman to win the Nobel.

question_en: What does geology study?
context_en: Geology studies the composition, structure and history of the Earth. It includes plate tectonics, minerals and fossils.

question_en: What is a black hole?
options_en: A region of space with gravity so intense that nothing can escape
context_en: A black hole is a region where gravity is so intense that not even light can escape. They form when massive stars collapse.

question_en: What is the most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere?
context_en: Nitrogen (N₂) makes up 78% of the atmosphere. Oxygen makes up 21%. The rest are noble gases and CO₂.

question_en: How many seconds are there in a year?
context_en: A year has 365.25 days × 24 h × 3600 s ≈ 31.5 million seconds. That is why every 4 years there is a leap year.

question_en: What is gravity according to Newton?
options_en: A force of repulsion between masses
A force of attraction between masses
context_en: Newton stated in 1687 that gravity is a force of attraction between masses, proportional to them and inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

question_en: What is a stem cell?
options_en: A cell that can differentiate into different cell types
The first cell formed in an embryo
A type of red blood cell
context_en: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells capable of renewing themselves and differentiating into specialized cells. They are key in regenerative medicine.

question_en: Which law states 'for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction'?
context_en: Newton's third law (the action-reaction principle) explains why rockets work: they push gases backward and move forward.

question_en: What is Avogadro's number approximately?
context_en: Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³) is the amount of atoms, molecules or particles in one mole of any substance.

question_en: What is nuclear fusion?
options_en: The splitting of an atomic nucleus
The joining of two nuclei to release energy
The fission process in nuclear power plants
context_en: Nuclear fusion joins light nuclei (hydrogen) to form heavier ones (helium), releasing enormous energy. It is the Sun's energy source.

question_en: Which vitamin does the body produce with sunlight?
context_en: The skin synthesizes vitamin D when exposed to the sun's UVB rays. It is essential for absorbing calcium and keeping bones healthy.

question_en: What is a tsunami?
options_en: A giant wave caused by underwater earthquakes
context_en: A tsunami is a series of ocean waves caused by earthquakes, volcanic eruptions or underwater landslides. They can travel at 800 km/h.

question_en: What is the greenhouse effect?
options_en: The warming of the Earth by gases that trap solar heat
context_en: The greenhouse effect is the warming of the Earth because gases like CO₂ and water vapor trap solar heat. Without the natural effect, the Earth would be uninhabitable.

question_en: What is the Big Bang?
options_en: The theory about the origin of the universe ~13.8 billion years ago
The expansion of the universe in the future
context_en: The Big Bang was the initial event of the universe ~13.8 billion years ago. The universe began in a state of infinite density and temperature and has expanded since then.

question_en: What is quantum mechanics?
options_en: The physics that describes the behavior of subatomic particles
context_en: Quantum mechanics describes the behavior of subatomic particles like electrons and photons. It includes concepts like wave-particle duality and the uncertainty principle.

question_en: What is the symbol for iron on the periodic table?
context_en: Iron is represented as Fe, from the Latin 'ferrum'. It has atomic number 26 and is the most abundant metal on Earth.

question_en: What is Einstein's special theory of relativity?
options_en: The speed of light is constant and time is relative
All motion is relative to the observer
context_en: Special relativity (1905) states that the speed of light is constant and that E=mc². Time and space are relative to the observer. There is no absolute space or time.

question_en: What is mitosis?
options_en: The cell division that produces two identical cells
context_en: Mitosis is the cell division process that produces two daughter cells with the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell. It is the basis of growth and repair.

question_en: What is mitochondrial DNA?
options_en: The DNA of the cell nucleus
The DNA of the mitochondria, inherited only through the maternal line
context_en: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited exclusively through the maternal line. It is used in population genetics to trace ancestors. 'Mitochondrial Eve' is the common maternal ancestor.

question_en: Which planet has the most visible rings?
context_en: Saturn's rings are the most visible, composed mainly of ice and rock. They were discovered by Galileo in 1610 (although he could not identify them).

question_en: What is the ozone layer?
options_en: A layer of CO₂ in the stratosphere
A layer of ozone gas in the stratosphere that filters UV rays
The outermost layer of the atmosphere
A layer of particles in the troposphere
context_en: The ozone layer is in the stratosphere (15-35 km). It absorbs 97-99% of the Sun's ultraviolet radiation. CFCs damaged it, but it is recovering since the Montreal Protocol.

question_en: What is natural selection?
options_en: The process by which living beings choose a mate
The evolutionary mechanism by which the best adapted survive and reproduce
context_en: Darwin's natural selection explains how the best-adapted organisms survive and pass on their traits. It is the main mechanism of evolution.

question_en: What is the heaviest element on the periodic table?
context_en: Oganesson (Og, element 118) is the heaviest of the confirmed elements. It is synthetic, radioactive and was first synthesized in 2002.

question_en: What is atmospheric pressure?
options_en: The weight of the column of air acting on a surface
context_en: Atmospheric pressure is the weight of the air on a surface. At sea level it is ~101,325 Pa (1 atm). It decreases with altitude, which is why water boils sooner in the mountains.

question_en: What did Alexander Graham Bell invent?
context_en: Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1876. However, Elisha Gray filed a similar design the same day. The invention's authorship was disputed.

question_en: What is thermodynamics?
options_en: The study of heat and its transformation into other forms of energy
context_en: Thermodynamics studies the relationships between heat, work and energy. Its four laws describe how energy behaves in physical systems.

question_en: What is the Pythagorean theorem?
options_en: The sum of the angles of a triangle is 180°
In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the legs
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter
context_en: The Pythagorean theorem (a²+b²=c²) describes the relationship between the sides of a right triangle. It was known in Babylon and India before Greece.

question_en: What is an atom?
options_en: The basic unit of matter, made up of a nucleus and electrons
context_en: The atom is the basic unit of matter. It has a nucleus (protons and neutrons) surrounded by electrons. It was proposed by Dalton in 1803; its structure was described by Rutherford in 1911.

question_en: What did Isaac Newton discover under an apple tree (according to legend)?
context_en: The apple tree story (possibly real) led Newton to formulate the law of universal gravitation (1687). According to Voltaire, Newton's niece told it.

question_en: What is photovoltaics?
options_en: The production of heat through light
The conversion of sunlight into electricity
context_en: Photovoltaic energy converts sunlight into electricity using silicon solar cells. The photoelectric effect was explained by Einstein (Nobel 1921).

question_en: What is the fastest animal in the world?
context_en: The peregrine falcon (Falco peregrinus) reaches ~389 km/h in a dive. The cheetah is the fastest on land (110 km/h) and the blue marlin in the sea.

question_en: How long does the Earth take to orbit the Sun?
context_en: The Earth takes 365.25 days to orbit the Sun. That is why every 4 years there is a leap year (366 days). The exact year is 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes and 46 seconds.

question_en: What is nanotechnology?
options_en: The technology that works at the nanometric scale (millionths of a mm)
context_en: Nanotechnology works at a scale of 1-100 nanometers (millionths of a millimeter). It has applications in medicine, electronics and advanced materials.

question_en: What is artificial intelligence?
options_en: The ability of machines to perform tasks that require human intelligence
The simulation of the human brain
context_en: AI is the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, especially computer systems. It includes machine learning, neural networks and language processing.

question_en: Which subatomic particle has a negative charge?
context_en: The electron has a negative charge and orbits the atomic nucleus. The proton has a positive charge and the neutron is neutral. The positron is the electron's antimatter.

question_en: What is CRISPR?
context_en: CRISPR-Cas9 is a gene-editing tool that acts like 'molecular scissors'. It allows DNA to be modified with great precision. Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2020.

question_en: How many galaxies are estimated to be in the observable universe?
context_en: It is estimated that there are ~2 trillion (2×10¹²) galaxies in the observable universe. The Milky Way contains between 200 and 400 billion stars.

question_en: What is nuclear fission energy?
options_en: The joining of light atomic nuclei
The splitting of heavy nuclei that releases energy
context_en: Nuclear fission splits heavy nuclei (uranium, plutonium), releasing great energy. It is the basis of nuclear reactors and the atomic bomb.

question_en: How long does sunlight take to reach the Earth?
context_en: Light takes ~8 minutes and 20 seconds to travel the 150 million km to Earth. This means we see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago.

question_en: What is the number π (pi)?
options_en: The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter
context_en: Pi (π ≈ 3.14159...) is the ratio of the circumference to the diameter of any circle. It is an irrational and transcendental number, with infinite decimals with no pattern.

question_en: What is the chemical symbol for sodium?
context_en: Sodium has the symbol 'Na', from the Latin 'natrium'. It is a very reactive alkali metal.

question_en: What is the most abundant element in the Earth's crust?
context_en: Oxygen makes up approximately 46% by mass of the Earth's crust, followed by silicon (28%) and aluminum (8%).

question_en: Which unit measures electric current?
context_en: The ampere (A) measures electric current. Its name honors the French physicist André-Marie Ampère.

question_en: Which unit measures pressure in the SI?
context_en: The pascal (Pa = N/m²) is the pressure unit of the International System. Standard atmospheric pressure is 101,325 Pa.

question_en: Which unit measures frequency?
context_en: The hertz (Hz) measures cycles per second. It is named after the physicist Heinrich Hertz.

question_en: Which subatomic particle has a positive charge?
context_en: The proton has a positive charge (+1) and is found in the nucleus of the atom.

question_en: Who formulated the laws of motion of bodies?
context_en: Isaac Newton formulated the three laws of motion in his 'Principia Mathematica' (1687).

question_en: Which property describes the amount of matter in a body?
context_en: Mass is the amount of matter. It is measured in kilograms. It is not the same as weight (a force).

question_en: What is an isotope?
options_en: An atom with the same atomic number, different mass number
context_en: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons.

question_en: What type of bond does water form between its molecules?
context_en: Water molecules attract each other through hydrogen bonds, which explains many of their unusual properties.

question_en: How many chemical elements are noble gases?
context_en: The noble gases are 6 classic elements: helium, neon, argon, krypton, xenon and radon. Oganesson (the 7th) was synthesized in 2002.

question_en: Which particle is responsible for transmitting the electromagnetic force?
context_en: The photon is the mediating particle of the electromagnetic interaction. It is the 'particle' of light.

question_en: Who discovered the electron?
context_en: J.J. Thomson discovered the electron in 1897 through experiments with cathode rays. Nobel Prize in Physics 1906.

question_en: Who proposed the atomic model with a central nucleus?
context_en: Ernest Rutherford proposed the nuclear model of the atom in 1911 after his famous experiment with gold foils.

question_en: Which phenomenon does the Doppler effect describe?
context_en: The Doppler effect explains the apparent variation in the frequency of a wave when there is relative motion between source and observer.

question_en: What is entropy?
context_en: In thermodynamics, entropy measures the degree of disorder of a system. The 2nd law says it tends to increase.

question_en: Which formula sums up energy-mass in relativity?
context_en: Einstein's famous equation E=mc² shows the equivalence between mass and energy. c is the speed of light.

question_en: Who stated the uncertainty principle?
context_en: Werner Heisenberg formulated the uncertainty principle in 1927: one cannot precisely know both the position and momentum of a particle at the same time.

question_en: Which equation describes the quantum wave?
context_en: The Schrödinger equation (1926) describes the temporal evolution of the wave function of a quantum system.

question_en: Which unit measures the amount of substance?
context_en: The mole is the SI unit of amount of substance. One mole contains 6.022 × 10²³ elementary entities.

question_en: What is the pH of pure water at 25 °C?
context_en: Pure water has a pH of 7 at 25 °C, considered neutral. Below that it is acidic and above it is basic.

question_en: What type of mixture is air?
context_en: Air is a homogeneous mixture of gases: ~78% nitrogen, ~21% oxygen and traces of other gases.

question_en: What is sublimation?
options_en: The change from solid to liquid
The change from solid to gas
The change from liquid to gas
The change from gas to solid
context_en: Sublimation is the direct change from solid to gas, without passing through the liquid phase. E.g.: dry ice (solid CO₂).

question_en: Which metal is liquid at room temperature?
context_en: Mercury (Hg) is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature. Gallium also melts at ~30 °C.

question_en: How many faces does a dodecahedron have?
context_en: The regular dodecahedron has 12 pentagonal faces, 20 vertices and 30 edges. It is one of the five Platonic solids.

question_en: How many faces does an icosahedron have?
context_en: The regular icosahedron has 20 triangular faces, 12 vertices and 30 edges. It is one of the Platonic solids.

question_en: How many faces does an octahedron have?
context_en: The regular octahedron has 8 triangular faces, 6 vertices and 12 edges.

question_en: What is the number e (Euler's constant) approximately?
context_en: The number e ≈ 2.71828 is the base of the natural logarithm and appears in many contexts of exponential growth.

question_en: Which number is called the 'golden ratio'?
context_en: The golden ratio φ ≈ 1.618 appears in many contexts of art, nature and mathematics.

question_en: Which theorem relates the sides of a right triangle?
context_en: The Pythagorean theorem: in a right triangle, a² + b² = c² (where c is the hypotenuse).

question_en: What is the formula for the area of a circle?
context_en: The area of a circle is A = π × r². The circumference is C = 2πr.

question_en: How many degrees do the interior angles of a triangle add up to?
context_en: The sum of the interior angles of any triangle (in Euclidean geometry) is 180°.

question_en: How many degrees do the interior angles of a quadrilateral add up to?
context_en: The interior angles of a quadrilateral add up to 360°. In general, for an n-sided polygon: (n-2) × 180°.

question_en: What is the smallest prime number?
context_en: 2 is the first prime number and the only even prime. 1 is not considered prime by convention.

question_en: Who is considered the father of geometry?
context_en: Euclid (3rd century BC) wrote 'The Elements', a foundational work of classical geometry.

question_en: Who created infinitesimal calculus independently besides Newton?
context_en: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz developed calculus independently of Newton. His notation (dx/dy, ∫) is the one used today.

question_en: What is a supermassive black hole?
options_en: A black hole of millions of solar masses
context_en: Supermassive black holes, with masses of millions to billions of suns, are found at the center of most galaxies.

question_en: How much heat is needed to raise one gram of water by 1 °C?
context_en: The calorie was defined as the heat needed to raise one gram of water by 1 °C. It is equivalent to ~4.184 joules.

question_en: Which hormone regulates blood sugar?
context_en: Insulin, produced in the pancreas, regulates blood glucose by allowing glucose to enter cells.

question_en: What is cold fusion?
context_en: Cold fusion would be a hypothetical nuclear fusion at room temperature. Today there is no reproducible evidence to demonstrate it.

question_en: What is dark matter?
options_en: Matter that does not emit light
context_en: Dark matter is a hypothetical form of matter that does not interact electromagnetically. It makes up ~27% of the universe.

question_en: What is antimatter?
context_en: Antimatter is made up of antiparticles (with opposite charge to normal ones). When it meets matter, it annihilates, releasing energy.

question_en: Which unit measures energy in the SI?
context_en: The joule (J) is the unit of energy and work in the SI. 1 J = 1 N·m.

question_en: Which type of radiation has the longest wavelength?
context_en: Among the options, microwaves have the longest wavelength (cm-mm). Gamma rays have the shortest.

question_en: Who said 'E pur si muove' according to tradition?
context_en: According to tradition, Galileo Galilei said 'And yet it moves' after recanting the heliocentric theory before the Inquisition.

question_en: What did Mendeleev discover in 1869?
context_en: Dmitri Mendeleev published the first periodic table in 1869, ordering the elements by their chemical properties.

question_en: Which is the element with atomic number 1?
context_en: Hydrogen (H) has atomic number 1: one proton. It is the most abundant element in the universe.

question_en: How many protons does a carbon atom have?
context_en: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number 6). The most common isotope is ¹²C, with 6 protons and 6 neutrons.

question_en: Which unit measures absorbed radiation?
context_en: The gray (Gy) measures the absorbed dose of ionizing radiation. The sievert (Sv) measures the equivalent dose (biological effects).

question_en: Who discovered radioactivity?
context_en: Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in uranium salts in 1896. He shared the Nobel with the Curies in 1903.

question_en: Which element did Marie and Pierre Curie discover in 1898 along with polonium?
context_en: The Curies isolated radium (Ra) and polonium (Po, named after Poland) in 1898 from pitchblende.

question_en: Which property describes the expansion of a body when heated?
context_en: The coefficient of expansion describes how much the volume or length of a material changes for each degree of temperature variation.

question_en: Which phenomenon produces the rainbow?
context_en: The rainbow forms through the refraction, internal reflection and dispersion of sunlight in water drops.

question_en: Which unit measures power?
context_en: The watt (W) is the unit of power in the SI. 1 W = 1 J/s.

question_en: At what temperature does ice melt at atmospheric pressure?
context_en: Water melts (and freezes) at 0 °C at 1 atm of pressure. It is the lower reference point of the Celsius scale.

question_en: What type of wave is sound?
context_en: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave that requires a material medium (air, water, solids) to propagate.

question_en: How fast does sound travel in air (approx.)?
context_en: Sound travels at ~343 m/s in air at 20 °C. In water, ~1,480 m/s; in steel, ~5,000 m/s.

question_en: Which property describes the opposition to the flow of electric current?
context_en: Electrical resistance, measured in ohms (Ω), describes the opposition to the flow of current. Ohm's law: V = I × R.

question_en: Who stated Ohm's law?
context_en: Georg Simon Ohm formulated his law in 1827, relating voltage, current and resistance: V = I · R.

question_en: What did Alessandro Volta invent?
context_en: Volta invented the voltaic pile in 1800, the first electric battery. The unit of voltage, the volt, is named after him.

question_en: Which law describes the attraction between two masses?
context_en: Newton's law of universal gravitation: F = G · m₁ · m₂ / r².

question_en: What type of rock is granite?
context_en: Granite is an intrusive (plutonic) igneous rock formed by the slow cooling of magma beneath the surface.

question_en: What is the name of the supercontinent that existed ~250 million years ago?
context_en: Pangaea was the supercontinent that grouped almost all the land mass. It fragmented into the present-day continents.

question_en: How many major tectonic plates are there?
context_en: Seven major tectonic plates are recognized: African, Antarctic, Eurasian, North American, South American, Indo-Australian and Pacific.

question_en: Which internal organ is in charge of pumping blood?
context_en: The heart pumps about 5 liters of blood per minute at rest. It beats around 100,000 times a day.

question_en: What is the name of the tube that connects the mouth to the stomach?
context_en: The esophagus is the muscular tube that transports food from the pharynx to the stomach through peristalsis.

question_en: Which gas does the human mainly exhale?
context_en: When we breathe, we mainly exhale nitrogen (which comes in and out equally), but the gas that increases is CO₂, a product of metabolism.

question_en: What function does hemoglobin have?
context_en: Hemoglobin is a protein present in red blood cells that transports oxygen from the lungs to the tissues.

question_en: What is the normal human body temperature?
context_en: Normal body temperature ranges between 36.5 and 37.5 °C in healthy adults.

question_en: What is the heliocentric theory?
context_en: The heliocentric theory places the Sun at the center of the solar system. It was proposed by Copernicus (1543) and defended by Galileo and Kepler.

question_en: What is the geocentric theory?
options_en: The Sun revolves around the Earth
The Earth revolves around the Sun
context_en: The geocentric theory placed the Earth motionless at the center of the universe. It was defended by Ptolemy and dominated until the 16th century.

question_en: How many laws describe Kepler's planetary motion?
context_en: Kepler's three laws describe the elliptical orbits, the swept areas and the period of the planets around the Sun.

question_en: Who discovered nuclear fission?
context_en: Otto Hahn discovered nuclear fission in 1938 (along with Strassmann and Meitner). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1944.

question_en: Which particle completed the Standard Model when it was discovered in 2012?
context_en: The Higgs boson was confirmed at CERN's LHC in 2012, validating a mechanism proposed in 1964 to explain mass.

question_en: Which fundamental force is responsible for beta radioactive decay?
context_en: The weak nuclear force is responsible for beta decay, where a neutron transforms into a proton by emitting an electron.

question_en: How many fundamental forces does physics know of?
context_en: The four fundamental forces are: gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear and weak nuclear.

question_en: Who designed the first vaccine (smallpox)?
context_en: Edward Jenner developed the first vaccine in 1796 using cowpox to immunize against human smallpox.

question_en: Who developed pasteurization?
context_en: Louis Pasteur developed this preservation method in the 19th century. He also worked on vaccines against rabies.

question_en: Which cell has no nucleus in maturity?
context_en: Mature mammalian erythrocytes (red blood cells) lose their nucleus to carry more hemoglobin.

question_en: What is the first law of thermodynamics?
context_en: The first law of thermodynamics states that energy is neither created nor destroyed, only transformed.

question_en: What does the 2nd law of thermodynamics describe?
context_en: The 2nd law states that in an isolated system, total entropy always tends to increase over time.

question_en: Which unit measures magnetic induction in the SI?
context_en: The tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic flux density. 1 T = 10,000 gauss.

question_en: Which famous scientist predicted gravitational waves in 1916?
context_en: Einstein predicted gravitational waves as a consequence of his theory of general relativity. They were first detected in 2015.

question_en: Which equations describe classical electromagnetism?
context_en: Maxwell's four equations (1865) unify electricity and magnetism and predict the existence of electromagnetic waves.

question_en: What is the escape velocity of the Earth (approx.)?
context_en: The escape velocity from the Earth's surface is ~11.2 km/s. Below that value an object falls back down.

question_en: Who discovered the neutron?
context_en: James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932. Nobel Prize in Physics 1935.

question_en: Which SI unit measures the plane angle?
context_en: The radian (rad) is the SI unit of plane angle. A full turn is equivalent to 2π radians (360°).

question_en: What is the Sun's main energy source?
context_en: The Sun gets its energy from the thermonuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium in its core.

question_en: Which property of light is explained by quantum theory?
context_en: The photoelectric effect, explained by Einstein in 1905 with quanta of light, earned him the Nobel Prize in Physics 1921.

question_en: What did Galileo invent (among other things)?
context_en: Galileo improved the telescope in 1609 and used it to observe the moons of Jupiter, the phases of Venus and sunspots.

question_en: Which temperature represents absolute zero?
context_en: Absolute zero is -273.15 °C, equivalent to 0 K. It is the lowest temperature theoretically possible.

question_en: Which famous book did Darwin write in 1859?
context_en: 'On the Origin of Species' (1859) presented the theory of evolution by natural selection.

question_en: Which cell transmits nerve impulses?
context_en: The neuron is the basic cell of the nervous system. It has dendrites, a cell body and an axon.
